original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded Array in createVestings Leads to Out-of-Gas DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The createVestings function iterates over an unbounded array of beneficiaries, performing state updates and external token transfers for each. A large array will exceed the block gas limit.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Token issuers attempting to distribute vestings to hundreds of users in a single transaction will fail. Since the function pulls tokens via safeTransferFrom in a loop, it is highly gas-intensive.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for loop over _beneficiaries.length calling _createVesting which does safeTransferFrom

preconditions: Token issuer calls createVestings with a large array (e.g., > 100 addresses)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: O(n) external calls in a single transaction
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Transaction reverts due to block gas limit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but operational failure for the protocol

economic_questions: What is the maximum safe array size on target chains (Ethereum, Arbitrum)?
Does the deployer contract batching exacerbate this?
validation_requirements: Measure gas cost per beneficiary in createVestings

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Forces issuers to write custom batching scripts

validation_result.impact_summary: The createVestings function performs O(n) external safeTransferFrom calls in an unbounded loop, which will exceed block gas limits for sufficiently large arrays, causing transaction reverts and wasted gas for privileged callers.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The createVestings function in SecondSwap_StepVesting.sol iterates over an unbounded array of beneficiaries. For each beneficiary, _createVesting is called with _isInternal=false, which triggers a token.safeTransferFrom external call. Each iteration involves at minimum: (1) storage reads/writes to _vestings mapping (~20k+ gas for cold SSTORE), (2) an external safeTransferFrom call (~30-60k gas depending on token implementation), and (3) event emission. This totals roughly 80-120k gas per beneficiary. On Ethereum mainnet with a 30M gas limit, this caps practical batch sizes at roughly 250-375 beneficiaries. On L2s like Arbitrum, limits may differ. The code lacks any upper bound check on _beneficiaries.length. While the callers are privileged (tokenIssuer, manager, or vestingDeployer), and they CAN work around this by calling with smaller batches, the missing check constitutes a code defect. Additionally, the VestingDeployer.createVestings function simply forwards the entire array to StepVesting.createVestings, providing no batching assistance.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is correct. Lines 176-184 of SecondSwap_StepVesting.sol show the unbounded loop. Each iteration calls _createVesting (line 181) which at lines 216-217 performs token.safeTransferFrom(tokenIssuer, address(this), _totalAmount) when _isInternal is false. This is an O(n) external call pattern with no array length bound.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No upper bound check on _beneficiaries.length. The loop performs external safeTransferFrom calls per iteration, making it highly gas-intensive and susceptible to block gas limit reverts.
Each call to _createVesting with _isInternal=false performs an external ERC20 safeTransferFrom, making each loop iteration gas-expensive (~80-120k gas per beneficiary including storage writes).

calling_sequence: 1. Caller (tokenIssuer/manager/vestingDeployer) calls createVestings with arrays of beneficiaries and amounts
4. Loop begins iterating over all beneficiaries (line 180)
5. For each i: _createVesting(_beneficiaries[i], _totalAmounts[i], 0, false) is called (line 181)
9. Loop continues until all beneficiaries processed or gas exhausted
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The complete loop at lines 180-182 has no maximum iteration bound. Each iteration performs at minimum one cold SSTORE (~20k gas), one external safeTransferFrom call (~30-60k gas), and one event emission (~1.5k gas). With ~100k gas per iteration conservatively, the theoretical max on Ethereum mainnet (30M gas limit) is ~300 beneficiaries, but accounting for base transaction cost and other overhead, practical limits are lower. The VestingDeployer.createVestings (SecondSwap_VestingDeployer.sol:173-182) also has an unbounded loop emitting events after forwarding the call, adding further gas overhead.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts directly constrain array sizes. The deployment scripts show vesting creation via VestingDeployer which forwards to StepVesting.createVestings without any batching logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an adversarial attack but an operational failure scenario. A token issuer attempting to distribute vestings to a large set of beneficiaries (e.g., airdrop to 500+ recipients) in a single transaction will have the transaction revert, wasting gas. The issuer must independently determine a safe batch size through trial and error or gas estimation. There is no protocol-level guidance or enforcement of safe batch sizes.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The defect is a missing bounds check that prevents operational failure. Impact is limited because: (1) only privileged callers are affected, (2) the workaround (smaller batches) is straightforward, (3) no funds are at risk. However, failed transactions waste gas, and the lack of any max length creates an unnecessary footgun for integrators and token issuers.
recommended_mitigations: Add a maximum array length constant (e.g., MAX_BATCH_SIZE = 100) and require(_beneficiaries.length <= MAX_BATCH_SIZE) at the start of createVestings
Consider aggregating all token transfers into a single safeTransferFrom for the total amount, then distributing internally without per-iteration external calls
Document recommended batch sizes for callers in function NatSpec comments
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy StepVesting with a test token. Call createVestings with increasing array sizes (50, 100, 200, 500) while measuring gas consumption. Identify the exact threshold where transactions revert on target chains (Ethereum mainnet 30M limit, Arbitrum). Compare gas cost per beneficiary to confirm ~80-120k gas estimate.
uncertainties: Exact gas cost per iteration depends on token implementation (standard ERC20 vs. more complex tokens)
L2 gas limits may be higher than mainnet, partially mitigating the issue on chains like Arbitrum
Whether the protocol intends this function for small batches only (though no documentation suggests this)
additional_testing_needed: Gas profiling of createVestings with varying array sizes on target deployment chains
Comparison of gas costs between individual safeTransferFrom per beneficiary vs. aggregated transfer approach
Testing whether VestingDeployer.createVestings adds significant overhead from its own event emission loop
